votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the recursive logic of tri_elem and accurately translates the list comprehension in tri_spec.

The Python code correctly implements the base cases and recursive logic of the Coq Fixpoint, including the even/odd branching and integer division. The specification function correctly generates the expected list for comparison.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq Fixpoint logic. The Coq pattern matching handles n=0 and n=1 explicitly, making the inner p=0 case unreachable; Python mirrors this with if/elif/else. The recursive step and arithmetic operations (integer division, parity check) are identical.

The Python code correctly translates the Coq specification. The tri_elem function handles the same base cases (n=0 returns 1, n=1 returns 3) and recursive cases. For n>=2, it checks evenness and computes the same values: for even n, returns 1 + n//2 (matching Nat.div2); for odd n, recursively computes tri_elem(n-1) + tri_elem(n-2) + (1 + (n+1)//2). The tri_spec function checks if l equals the list of tri_elem values from 0 to n, matching the Coq spec's use of map tri_elem (seq 0 (n+1)).

The Python code correctly translates the Coq specification. The tri_elem function handles the same base cases (n=0 returns 1, n=1 returns 3) and recursive cases. For n>=2, it checks evenness and computes either 1 + n//2 (for even n) or tri_elem(n-1) + tri_elem(n-2) + (1 + (n+1)//2) (for odd n). Nat.div2 in Coq performs integer division by 2, which matches Python's // 2. The tri_spec function checks if the list equals the map of tri_elem over [0..n], which the Python list comprehension correctly implements. The Prop vs bool difference is acceptable since we're checking concrete equality.

The Python code correctly translates the Coq specification. The tri_elem function handles base cases (n=0 returns 1, n=1 returns 3) identically. For n>=2, the Coq code checks Nat.even n and uses Nat.div2 for integer division by 2, which Python replicates with n % 2 == 0 and n // 2. The recursive case for odd n computes tri_elem(p) + tri_elem(q) + (1 + (n+1)//2) where p=n-1 and q=n-2, matching the Coq logic. The tri_spec function checks if l equals the list of tri_elem values from 0 to n, which matches the Coq Prop that l = map tri_elem (seq 0 (n+1)).

tri_elem matches Coq on naturals: base cases 0->1, 1->3; for n>=2, even case returns 1+div2 n, odd case recurses on n-1 and n-2 and adds 1+div2(n+1). tri_spec compares l to [tri_elem(i) for i in 0..n]. Same concrete results for all natural inputs.
Python mirrors Coq on naturals: same base cases, even/odd handling, recursive calls with p=n-1 and q=n-2, and tri_spec compares l to [tri_elem(i) for i in 0..n].
tri_elem matches Coq for n>=0 (base cases, even/odd branches, div2) and tri_spec builds the same list via range/seq; concrete outputs coincide.
